items.title: How pharmacokinetics and pharmacodynamics partition the variability in drug action
items.content:
Variability in drug response is not inevitable randomness; it can be analyzed.
The chain between giving a drug and producing an effect divides into two components.
Pharmacokinetics describes the relationship between drug concentration and time — the processes determining drug delivery to, and removal from, molecular targets.
Pharmacodynamics describes the relationship between drug concentration and effect — variability in drug action independent of delivery to effector sites.
keyPoints: Pharmacokinetics = concentration vs time (delivery/removal: ADME)
Pharmacodynamics = concentration vs effect (independent of delivery)
Both contribute to inter-individual variability in drug action
items.reference: Principles of Clinical Pharmacology: "The resulting description of the relationship between drug concentration and time is termed pharmacokinetics... This description of the relationship between drug concentration and effect is termed pharmacodynamics."

items.title: Why 'adherence' is preferred to 'compliance'
items.content:
Word choice in describing whether patients take their drugs carries an implicit judgment.
Across all cultures and diseases, adherence, genetic variants affecting pharmacokinetics/pharmacodynamics, and drug interactions contribute to drug responses.
The term 'adherence' is preferred to the older term 'compliance' because it removes the idea that the patient is at fault.
Cost and cultural factors also influence which drugs, combinations, or OTC remedies are used.
keyPoints: 'Adherence' is preferred over 'compliance' — it does not blame the patient
Adherence, genetic variants, and drug interactions all shape response
Cost/cultural factors influence prescribing and OTC use
items.reference: GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS: "the term \"adherence\" is preferred to the older term \"compliance\" because it removes the idea that the patient is at fault."

items.title: How the therapeutic window guides whether to monitor plasma concentrations
items.content:
Beneficial and adverse effects are described by dose-response relations.
Well-tolerated drugs have a wide margin — the therapeutic ratio, index, or window — between doses that produce a therapeutic effect and those producing toxicity.
When there is an established relationship between plasma concentration and effect, monitoring concentrations helps keep levels above the minimum effective and below the toxic range.
This is the rationale for therapeutic drug monitoring of agents with a narrow window.
keyPoints: Therapeutic window = gap between effective and toxic doses/concentrations
A wide window means greater safety margin
Monitor plasma levels when an established concentration–effect relationship exists
items.reference: Therapeutic Index: "Well-tolerated drugs demonstrate a wide margin, termed the therapeutic ratio, therapeutic index, or therapeutic window, between the doses required to produce a therapeutic effect and those producing toxicity."

items.title: How drug disposition (ADME) sets the concentration at the target
items.content:
Pharmacokinetics is built on four disposition processes.
The processes of absorption, distribution, metabolism, and excretion — collectively termed drug disposition — determine the concentration of drug delivered to target effector molecules.
Each of these can be altered by disease, drug interactions, or genetic variants.
Understanding them allows rational dosing for any drug.
keyPoints: Disposition = Absorption, Distribution, Metabolism, Excretion (ADME)
Together they set the concentration at the molecular target
Disease, interactions, and genetics modify each step
items.reference: PRINCIPLES OF PHARMACOKINETICS: "The processes of absorption, distribution, metabolism, and excretion—collectively termed drug disposition—determine the concentration of drug delivered to target effector molecules."

items.title: Why bioavailability can be less than 100%
items.content:
Non-intravenous routes may deliver less drug to the systemic circulation than IV.
The fraction of drug available to the systemic circulation by non-IV routes is termed bioavailability.
Bioavailability may be <100% for two main reasons: incomplete absorption, or metabolism/elimination prior to entering the systemic circulation.
Compared with the same IV dose, a non-IV dose has a later and lower peak plasma concentration.
keyPoints: Bioavailability = fraction reaching systemic circulation by a non-IV route
Two causes of <100%: incomplete absorption, or presystemic metabolism/elimination
Non-IV dosing gives a later, lower peak than IV
items.reference: ABSORPTION AND BIOAVAILABILITY: "Bioavailability may be <100% for two main reasons: (1) incomplete absorption, or (2) metabolism or elimination prior to entering the systemic circulation."

items.title: How presystemic (first-pass) elimination reduces oral bioavailability
items.content:
An orally administered drug must run a gauntlet before reaching the circulation.
It must traverse the intestinal epithelium, the portal venous system, and the liver prior to entering the systemic circulation.
Within the enterocyte the drug may be metabolized or excreted back into the lumen, and in the hepatocyte it may be metabolized or excreted into bile — both reduce bioavailability.
This elimination in intestine and liver is termed presystemic elimination, presystemic extraction, or first-pass elimination.
keyPoints: Oral drug crosses gut epithelium → portal vein → liver before systemic circulation
Enterocyte/hepatocyte metabolism and efflux into lumen/bile cut bioavailability
This is presystemic/first-pass elimination
items.reference: "First-Pass" Effect: "This elimination in intestine and liver, which reduces the amount of drug delivered to the systemic circulation, is termed presystemic elimination, presystemic extraction, or first-pass elimination."

items.title: How P-glycoprotein limits drug bioavailability and brain penetration
items.content:
Membrane transport combines passive diffusion with active uptake and efflux.
P-glycoprotein, the product of the ABCB1 (MDR1) gene, is a widely studied efflux pump expressed on the apical enterocyte and the canalicular hepatocyte.
In both locations it serves as an efflux pump, limiting availability of drug to the systemic circulation.
P-glycoprotein-mediated efflux from cerebral capillaries limits drug brain penetration and is an important component of the blood-brain barrier.
keyPoints: P-glycoprotein (ABCB1/MDR1) is an efflux pump on enterocytes and hepatocytes
It limits systemic bioavailability by pumping drug back out
At cerebral capillaries it forms part of the blood-brain barrier
items.reference: DRUG TRANSPORT: "P-glycoprotein-mediated drug efflux from cerebral capillaries limits drug brain penetration and is an important component of the blood-brain barrier."

items.title: How Phase I and Phase II metabolism make drugs more excretable
items.content:
Metabolism generally converts drugs into more polar, more readily excreted compounds, predominantly in the liver.
Phase I metabolism involves chemical modification, most often oxidation by the cytochrome P450 (CYP) monooxygenase superfamily.
Phase II metabolism involves conjugation of endogenous compounds to drugs or metabolites, via glucuronyl-, acetyl-, sulfo-, and methyltransferases.
Drug metabolites may themselves exert important pharmacologic activity.
keyPoints: Phase I = chemical modification, usually CYP-mediated oxidation
Phase II = conjugation (glucuronyl/acetyl/sulfo/methyltransferases)
Metabolism usually increases polarity/excretability; metabolites can be active
items.reference: DRUG METABOLISM: "Phase I metabolism involves chemical modification, most often oxidation accomplished by members of the cytochrome P450 (CYP) monooxygenase superfamily. Phase II metabolism involves conjugation of specific endogenous compounds to drugs or their metabolites."

items.title: Why nitroglycerin cannot be given orally but verapamil can
items.content:
Extensive presystemic metabolism dictates the usable route and the dose.
Nitroglycerin cannot be used orally because it is completely extracted prior to reaching the systemic circulation, so it is given sublingually, transdermally, or intravascularly to bypass presystemic metabolism.
Some drugs with very extensive presystemic metabolism can still be given orally using much higher doses than IV — a typical IV verapamil dose is 1–5 mg versus a usual oral dose of 40–120 mg.
Low-dose aspirin exploits first-pass deacylation in the liver to inhibit portal-vein platelet cyclooxygenase while sparing the systemic circulation.
keyPoints: Nitroglycerin: complete presystemic extraction → not oral (use SL/transdermal/IV)
Verapamil: oral dose (40–120 mg) >> IV dose (1–5 mg) due to first-pass loss
Low-dose aspirin: hepatic first-pass deacylation gives platelet effect with systemic sparing
items.reference: Clinical Implications of Reduced Bioavailability: "Nitroglycerin cannot be used orally because it is completely extracted prior to reaching the systemic circulation."

items.title: How half-life determines the time to steady state after any dosing change
items.content:
Most elimination is first-order — the rate depends on the amount of drug present.
Half-life is the time required for 50% of a first-order process to be completed, and first-order processes are near-complete after four to five half-lives.
Critically, the elimination half-life is the sole determinant of the time required for steady-state plasma concentrations to be achieved after any change in dosing.
This applies to starting therapy, changing the dose or interval, and stopping the drug.
keyPoints: First-order: rate depends on amount of drug present
Steady state (and washout) reached in ~4–5 half-lives
Half-life is the SOLE determinant of time to a new steady state
items.reference: Clinical Implications of Half-Life Measurements: "it is also the sole determinant of the time required for steady-state plasma concentrations to be achieved after any change in drug dosing."

items.title: Why a loading dose speeds onset but not the time to steady state
items.content:
When the indication is urgent, a higher initial dose can raise concentrations quickly.
Loading dosages are used to achieve rapid elevations of drug concentration and therapeutic effects earlier than with chronic maintenance therapy.
Nevertheless, the time required for a true steady state to be achieved is still determined only by the elimination half-life.
Thus a loading dose changes how fast you reach a therapeutic level, not how fast the system reaches steady state.
keyPoints: Loading dose achieves therapeutic concentrations earlier
Time to true steady state still depends only on elimination half-life
Useful for urgent indications
items.reference: Loading Doses: "Nevertheless, the time required for a true steady state to be achieved is still determined only by the elimination half-life."

items.title: Why adenosine and midazolam exploit the distribution phase
items.content:
Sometimes the transient, distribution-driven concentration peak is the therapeutic point.
Midazolam's intravenous sedation depends on its rapid uptake by the brain during the distribution phase, with subsequent egress as equilibrium is achieved.
Adenosine must be administered as a rapid bolus for reentrant supraventricular tachycardia to prevent elimination by very rapid (t½ of seconds) uptake into erythrocytes and endothelial cells before it can reach the AV node.
These illustrate that distribution and redistribution — not just elimination — shape the time course of drug effect.
keyPoints: Midazolam: rapid brain uptake during distribution → quick sedation, then redistribution
Adenosine: rapid bolus needed (t½ seconds) to reach the AV node before clearance
Distribution/redistribution can govern onset and offset of effect
items.reference: Rate of Intravenous Drug Administration: "adenosine must be administered as a rapid bolus in the treatment of reentrant supraventricular tachycardias...to prevent elimination by very rapid (t_{1/2} of seconds) uptake into erythrocytes and endothelial cells before the drug can reach its clinical site of action, the atrioventricular node."

items.title: Why small changes in protein binding matter most for highly bound drugs
items.content:
Only unbound (free) drug can distribute to sites of action, so response tracks free, not total, concentration.
In chronic kidney or liver disease, protein binding may be decreased and drug actions increased; acute-phase reactants (MI, infection, surgery) can transiently increase binding and decrease efficacy.
These changes matter most for highly protein-bound drugs, because a small change has a large proportional effect — a decrease in binding from 99% to 98% doubles the free drug concentration from 1% to 2%.
For some drugs (e.g., phenytoin), monitoring free rather than total concentrations is useful.
keyPoints: Effect relates to FREE drug; renal/hepatic disease lowers binding (↑ free)
Highly bound drugs: 99%→98% binding doubles free drug (1%→2%)
Monitor free levels for drugs like phenytoin
items.reference: Clinical Implications of Altered Protein Binding: "a decrease in binding from 99 to 98% doubles the free drug concentration from 1 to 2%. For some drugs (e.g., phenytoin), monitoring free rather than total drug concentrations can be useful."

items.title: How clearance is defined and why altered clearance demands dose adjustment
items.content:
Clearance is the volume of body fluid cleared of drug per unit time (volume/time).
Genetic variants, drug interactions, or diseases that reduce drug-metabolizing enzyme or excretory activity decrease clearance and require a downward dose adjustment to avoid toxicity.
Conversely, factors that increase elimination-pathway function require increased dosage to maintain effect.
Metabolites may produce effects similar to, overlapping with, or distinct from the parent drug.
keyPoints: Clearance = volume/time cleared of drug
Reduced clearance → lower the dose; increased clearance → raise the dose
Active metabolites can add or alter effects
items.reference: DRUG ELIMINATION: "This defines clearance as volume/time."

items.title: How prodrugs require metabolism to become active
items.content:
Some drugs are administered inactive and depend on metabolism for their effect.
Prodrugs are inactive compounds that require metabolism to generate active metabolites that mediate drug effects.
Examples include many ACE inhibitors, the ARB losartan, the antineoplastic irinotecan, the antiestrogen tamoxifen, the analgesic codeine (whose active metabolite morphine underlies the opioid effect), and the antiplatelet drug clopidogrel.
Drug metabolism can also bioactivate procarcinogens and generate reactive metabolites that mediate certain adverse reactions.
keyPoints: Prodrug = inactive parent needing metabolic activation
Examples: many ACE inhibitors, losartan, irinotecan, tamoxifen, codeine→morphine, clopidogrel
Metabolism can also bioactivate procarcinogens / reactive metabolites
items.reference: Clinical Implications of Altered Clearance: "Prodrugs are inactive compounds that require metabolism to generate active metabolites that mediate the drug effects. Examples include many angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE) inhibitors, the angiotensin receptor blocker losartan, the antineoplastic irinotecan, the antiestrogen tamoxifen, the analgesic codeine...and the antiplatelet drug clopidogrel."

items.title: Why dependence on a single metabolic pathway creates high-risk pharmacokinetics
items.content:
Reliance on one pathway makes drug concentrations fragile to anything inhibiting that pathway.
When plasma concentrations of active drug depend exclusively on a single metabolic pathway, any condition that inhibits it — disease, genetic, or drug interaction — can cause dramatic changes in concentration and marked variability in action.
Two scenarios: variable bioactivation of a prodrug (e.g., reduced CYP2D6 prevents codeine analgesia; reduced CYP2C19 reduces clopidogrel's antiplatelet effect), and reliance of an active drug's elimination on a single pathway (inhibition → marked accumulation and, for narrow-window drugs, dose-related toxicity).
When multiple elimination pathways exist, loss of one is much less likely to have a large impact.
keyPoints: Single-pathway dependence = high-risk pharmacokinetics
Prodrug scenario: low CYP2D6 → no codeine analgesia; low CYP2C19 → weak clopidogrel
Active-drug scenario: blocked sole elimination → accumulation/toxicity (narrow window)
items.reference: THE CONCEPT OF HIGH-RISK PHARMACOKINETICS: "When plasma concentrations of active drug depend exclusively on a single metabolic pathway, any condition that inhibits that pathway (be it disease related, genetic, or due to a drug interaction) can lead to dramatic changes in drug concentrations and marked variability in drug action."

items.title: Why clopidogrel's effect outlasts its plasma half-life
items.content:
Effect duration is not always tied to how long the drug stays in plasma.
A drug's clinical effect can be delayed or prolonged relative to its concentration by pharmacokinetic or mechanistic factors.
The elimination half-life of clopidogrel in plasma is about 6 h, but its antiplatelet effect persists for the life of the platelet, 7–10 days, because it binds irreversibly to P2Y12 receptors.
Thus an irreversible mechanism decouples duration of effect from plasma half-life.
keyPoints: Clopidogrel plasma t½ ≈ 6 h, but antiplatelet effect lasts 7–10 days
Cause: irreversible P2Y12 binding for the life of the platelet
Irreversible mechanisms uncouple effect duration from plasma kinetics
items.reference: Pharmacodynamics (Time Course of Drug Action): "the elimination half-life of clopidogrel in plasma in most subjects is ~6 h, but the antiplatelet effect (which is due to irreversible binding to P2Y12 receptors) persists for the life of the platelet, 7–10 days."

items.title: Why abrupt β-blocker withdrawal can cause rebound hypertension or tachycardia
items.content:
Receptors adapt to chronic drug exposure, which has clinical consequences on stopping.
β-adrenergic blockers upregulate β-receptor density during chronic therapy.
While this does not usually cause resistance to the therapeutic effect, it may produce severe agonist-mediated effects (e.g., hypertension or tachycardia) if the blocking drug is abruptly withdrawn.
Receptors may be up- or downregulated by disease or by the drug itself, modulating the drug effect over time.
keyPoints: Chronic β-blockade upregulates β-receptor density
Abrupt withdrawal can unmask agonist effects (rebound hypertension/tachycardia)
Receptor up/downregulation makes drug effects vary over time
items.reference: Drug Effects May Be Disease Specific: "β-adrenergic blockers upregulate β-receptor density...it may produce severe agonist-mediated effects (e.g., hypertension or tachycardia) if the blocking drug is abruptly withdrawn."

items.title: How anticipated toxicity dictates the starting dose
items.content:
The severity of expected toxicity shapes how aggressively to start.
If side effects are minor, it may be acceptable to start at a dose highly likely to achieve efficacy and down-titrate if side effects occur.
However, this is rarely justified if the anticipated toxicity is serious or life-threatening; then it is more appropriate to initiate with the lowest dose that may produce the desired effect.
In cancer chemotherapy it is common practice to use maximally tolerated doses. A target drug effect should be defined when treatment is started, and a dose increase is justified only if toxicity is absent and the likelihood of serious toxicity small.
keyPoints: Minor toxicity: start effective, down-titrate; serious/life-threatening toxicity: start lowest possibly-effective dose
Cancer chemotherapy commonly uses maximally tolerated doses
Define the target effect at the start; escalate only if toxicity is absent/unlikely
items.reference: PRINCIPLES OF DOSE SELECTION: "However, this approach is rarely, if ever, justified if the anticipated toxicity is serious or life-threatening; in this circumstance, it is more appropriate to initiate therapy with the lowest dose that may produce a desired effect."

items.title: How sampling timing (trough vs peak) is matched to the monitoring goal
items.content:
When monitoring plasma concentrations, the timing of the sample is chosen for the question being asked.
To ensure maintained efficacy (e.g., anticonvulsants) and to avoid concentration-dependent toxicity such as vancomycin nephrotoxicity, concentration is measured at trough — at its lowest, just prior to a dose at steady state.
To catch a peak-related hazard, sampling targets the peak: the QT interval during sotalol or dofetilide is best evaluated at anticipated peak concentration (e.g., 1–2 h postdose at steady state).
For first-order drugs, average/max/min steady-state concentrations are linearly related to dosing rate, so the maintenance dose can be scaled by the desired/measured concentration ratio.
keyPoints: Trough (predose) sampling for efficacy and to limit vancomycin nephrotoxicity / anticonvulsant levels
Peak sampling for peak-related risks (QT on sotalol/dofetilide, ~1–2 h postdose)
First-order: steady-state concentration scales linearly with dose (adjust by ratio)
items.reference: Principles of Dose Selection: "Maintained high vancomycin levels carry a risk of nephrotoxicity, so dosages should be adjusted on the basis of plasma concentrations measured at trough (predose)."

items.question: A drug's plasma concentration-versus-time profile is being characterized to understand its delivery to and removal from target molecules. This description is termed:
options: Pharmacodynamics
Pharmacokinetics
Pharmacogenomics
Bioequivalence
items.explanation: Pharmacokinetics is the description of the relationship between drug concentration and time (delivery to and removal from targets). Pharmacodynamics is the relationship between concentration and effect.
items.reference: Principles of Clinical Pharmacology: "The resulting description of the relationship between drug concentration and time is termed pharmacokinetics."

items.question: An oral drug has a bioavailability of 40%. Which two mechanisms best explain a bioavailability below 100%?
options: Increased renal clearance and high protein binding
Incomplete absorption and presystemic (first-pass) metabolism/elimination
Zero-order kinetics and receptor downregulation
Rapid distribution and a large volume of distribution
items.explanation: Bioavailability may be <100% for two main reasons: incomplete absorption, or metabolism/elimination prior to entering the systemic circulation (first-pass effect).
items.reference: ABSORPTION AND BIOAVAILABILITY: "Bioavailability may be <100% for two main reasons: (1) incomplete absorption, or (2) metabolism or elimination prior to entering the systemic circulation."

items.question: Nitroglycerin is administered sublingually or transdermally rather than orally because:
options: It is destroyed by gastric acid
It is completely extracted presystemically before reaching the systemic circulation if given orally
It is too large to be absorbed across the gut
Oral administration causes severe nausea
items.explanation: Nitroglycerin cannot be used orally because it is completely extracted prior to reaching the systemic circulation; sublingual, transdermal, or intravascular routes bypass presystemic metabolism.
items.reference: Clinical Implications of Reduced Bioavailability: "Nitroglycerin cannot be used orally because it is completely extracted prior to reaching the systemic circulation."

items.question: Why is the usual oral dose of verapamil (40–120 mg) much larger than its intravenous dose (1–5 mg)?
options: Oral verapamil is a different salt with lower potency
Extensive presystemic (first-pass) metabolism removes much of the oral dose
Oral verapamil is eliminated by zero-order kinetics
Verapamil is highly protein bound only when given orally
items.explanation: Some drugs with very extensive presystemic metabolism can still be given orally using much higher doses than IV; verapamil is the cited example (1–5 mg IV vs 40–120 mg oral).
items.reference: Clinical Implications of Reduced Bioavailability: "a typical intravenous dose of verapamil is 1–5 mg, compared to a usual single oral dose of 40–120 mg."

items.question: P-glycoprotein, the product of the ABCB1 (MDR1) gene, contributes to the blood-brain barrier by:
options: Actively pumping drugs out of cerebral capillaries, limiting brain penetration
Metabolizing drugs within neurons
Increasing passive diffusion of drugs into the CNS
Binding drugs to plasma proteins
items.explanation: P-glycoprotein is an efflux pump; its drug efflux from cerebral capillaries limits brain penetration and is an important component of the blood-brain barrier.
items.reference: DRUG TRANSPORT: "P-glycoprotein-mediated drug efflux from cerebral capillaries limits drug brain penetration and is an important component of the blood-brain barrier."

items.question: Oxidation of a drug by the cytochrome P450 monooxygenase superfamily is an example of:
options: Phase II metabolism
Phase I metabolism
Renal tubular secretion
Conjugation
items.explanation: Phase I metabolism is chemical modification, most often CYP-mediated oxidation. Phase II metabolism is conjugation (glucuronyl-, acetyl-, sulfo-, methyltransferases).
items.reference: DRUG METABOLISM: "Phase I metabolism involves chemical modification, most often oxidation accomplished by members of the cytochrome P450 (CYP) monooxygenase superfamily."

items.question: A drug is started at a fixed maintenance dose. Approximately how many elimination half-lives are needed to reach steady-state plasma concentrations?
options: About 1 half-life
About 2 half-lives
About 4 to 5 half-lives
About 10 half-lives
items.explanation: First-order processes such as elimination are near-complete after four to five half-lives, and the elimination half-life is the sole determinant of the time to reach a new steady state.
items.reference: PLASMA HALF-LIFE: "In practice, first-order processes such as elimination are near-complete after four to five half-lives."

items.question: A loading dose is given for an urgent indication. What is its effect on the time required to reach true steady state?
options: It shortens the time to true steady state
It has no effect on the time to true steady state, which depends only on elimination half-life
It lengthens the time to true steady state
It eliminates the concept of steady state
items.explanation: A loading dose achieves therapeutic concentrations earlier, but the time required for a true steady state is still determined only by the elimination half-life.
items.reference: Loading Doses: "Nevertheless, the time required for a true steady state to be achieved is still determined only by the elimination half-life."

items.question: Adenosine must be given as a rapid intravenous bolus for reentrant supraventricular tachycardia because:
options: It is acid-labile
Its half-life is seconds (rapid uptake into red cells and endothelium), so slow delivery would clear it before it reaches the AV node
It is highly protein bound
It undergoes extensive hepatic first-pass metabolism
items.explanation: Adenosine has a t½ of seconds due to rapid uptake into erythrocytes and endothelial cells; a rapid bolus is required so it reaches its site of action, the AV node, before being eliminated.
items.reference: Rate of Intravenous Drug Administration: "adenosine must be administered as a rapid bolus...to prevent elimination by very rapid (t_{1/2} of seconds) uptake into erythrocytes and endothelial cells before the drug can reach its clinical site of action, the atrioventricular node."

items.question: For a drug that is 99% protein bound, a decrease in binding to 98% (e.g., in renal or liver disease) will:
options: Halve the free drug concentration
Have no clinically meaningful effect
Double the free drug concentration (from 1% to 2%)
Increase total drug concentration tenfold
items.explanation: Because only free drug is active, small binding changes have large proportional effects for highly bound drugs: 99%→98% binding doubles the free fraction from 1% to 2%. Monitoring free phenytoin is a practical example.
items.reference: Clinical Implications of Altered Protein Binding: "a decrease in binding from 99 to 98% doubles the free drug concentration from 1 to 2%."

items.question: Which of the following is a prodrug that requires metabolism to an active form?
options: Clopidogrel
Adenosine
Vancomycin
Nitroglycerin
items.explanation: Clopidogrel is a prodrug requiring metabolic activation (via CYP2C19). Other listed prodrug examples include many ACE inhibitors, losartan, irinotecan, tamoxifen, and codeine (to morphine).
items.reference: Clinical Implications of Altered Clearance: "Prodrugs are inactive compounds that require metabolism to generate active metabolites that mediate the drug effects... and the antiplatelet drug clopidogrel."

items.question: A poor CYP2D6 metabolizer receives codeine for pain but gets little analgesia. The best explanation is:
options: Codeine is directly active and CYP2D6 inactivates it
Reduced CYP2D6 activity prevents bioactivation of codeine to its active metabolite morphine
CYP2D6 increases renal excretion of codeine
Codeine is eliminated by zero-order kinetics
items.explanation: Codeine is a prodrug whose analgesia depends on conversion to morphine; decreased CYP2D6 activity prevents this bioactivation — a classic high-risk-pharmacokinetic scenario from single-pathway prodrug activation.
items.reference: THE CONCEPT OF HIGH-RISK PHARMACOKINETICS: "examples include decreased CYP2D6 activity, which prevents analgesia by codeine, and decreased CYP2C19 activity, which reduces the antiplatelet effects of clopidogrel."

items.question: Clopidogrel's plasma half-life is about 6 hours, yet its antiplatelet effect lasts 7–10 days. Why?
options: It accumulates in fat and redistributes slowly
It binds irreversibly to platelet P2Y12 receptors, so the effect lasts the life of the platelet
Its metabolite has a half-life of 10 days
Platelets cannot metabolize it
items.explanation: The antiplatelet effect persists for the life of the platelet (7–10 days) because clopidogrel binds irreversibly to P2Y12 receptors — an irreversible mechanism uncouples effect duration from plasma half-life.
items.reference: Pharmacodynamics (Time Course of Drug Action): "the antiplatelet effect (which is due to irreversible binding to P2Y12 receptors) persists for the life of the platelet, 7–10 days."

items.question: A patient on chronic β-blocker therapy abruptly stops the drug and develops tachycardia and hypertension. The mechanism is:
options: Downregulation of β-receptors during therapy
Upregulation of β-receptor density during chronic blockade, unmasked by withdrawal
Irreversible β-receptor binding
Zero-order elimination of the β-blocker
items.explanation: β-blockers upregulate β-receptor density during chronic therapy; abrupt withdrawal can produce severe agonist-mediated effects such as hypertension or tachycardia.
items.reference: Drug Effects May Be Disease Specific: "β-adrenergic blockers upregulate β-receptor density...it may produce severe agonist-mediated effects (e.g., hypertension or tachycardia) if the blocking drug is abruptly withdrawn."

items.question: For a drug eliminated by zero-order (saturable) kinetics, such as phenytoin, a small increase in dose may:
options: Produce a proportional, linear increase in plasma concentration
Produce a disproportionately large increase in plasma concentration
Decrease the plasma concentration
Have no effect on plasma concentration
items.explanation: Linear (first-order) scaling of dose to concentration does not apply to zero-order kinetics (fixed amount per time), where small dose increases produce disproportionate concentration increases; examples include aspirin and fluoxetine (and phenytoin).
items.reference: Concentration of Drugs in Plasma as a Guide to Therapy: "This does not apply to drugs eliminated by zero-order kinetics (fixed amount per unit time), where small dosage increases will produce disproportionate increases in plasma concentration; examples include aspirin and fluoxetine."

items.question: To minimize the risk of vancomycin nephrotoxicity while maintaining efficacy, plasma concentrations should be measured:
options: At peak, 1–2 hours after the dose
At trough (predose) at steady state
At any random time
Only after five half-lives have elapsed once
items.explanation: Maintained high vancomycin levels risk nephrotoxicity, so dosing is adjusted on trough (predose) concentrations. Trough sampling is also used for anticonvulsants to ensure a maintained therapeutic effect.
items.reference: Principles of Dose Selection: "Maintained high vancomycin levels carry a risk of nephrotoxicity, so dosages should be adjusted on the basis of plasma concentrations measured at trough (predose)."

items.question: When monitoring the QT interval during sotalol or dofetilide therapy, the electrocardiogram is best evaluated:
options: At trough, just before the next dose
At the time of anticipated peak plasma concentration (e.g., 1–2 h postdose at steady state)
Immediately after the first dose only
At any convenient time
items.explanation: Because QT prolongation is a peak-concentration hazard, the ECG is evaluated at the anticipated peak (about 1–2 h postdose at steady state). This contrasts with trough monitoring used for efficacy/nephrotoxicity.
items.reference: Principles of Dose Selection: "evaluating the electrocardiogram at the time of anticipated peak plasma concentration and effect (e.g., 1–2 h postdose at steady state) is most appropriate."

items.question: When the anticipated toxicity of a drug is serious or life-threatening, the most appropriate starting strategy is to:
options: Start at a dose highly likely to be effective and down-titrate if toxicity occurs
Initiate therapy with the lowest dose that may produce the desired effect
Use the maximally tolerated dose from the outset
Avoid defining a target effect until toxicity appears
items.explanation: Starting high and down-titrating is acceptable only for minor side effects; when toxicity is serious or life-threatening, it is more appropriate to begin with the lowest dose that may produce the desired effect.
items.reference: PRINCIPLES OF DOSE SELECTION: "it is more appropriate to initiate therapy with the lowest dose that may produce a desired effect."

items.question: A patient on chronic antihypertensive therapy shows no response. Besides wrong diagnosis or dose, which is a common explanation for failure of efficacy where measuring plasma concentration is especially useful?
options: Receptor upregulation
Decreased adherence, drug interactions, or degraded/expired drug
Zero-order kinetics
A wide therapeutic window
items.explanation: Explanations for failure of efficacy include drug interactions, decreased adherence, or unexpectedly low concentration from expired/degraded drug — situations where measuring plasma concentration can be especially useful. Adherence is a frequent problem with multidrug, multidose regimens.
items.reference: Failure of Efficacy: "Other explanations for failure of efficacy include drug interactions, decreased adherence, or unexpectedly low drug concentration due to administration of expired or degraded drug."

items.question: For a drug with first-order elimination, if you wish to double the steady-state plasma concentration, you should:
options: Double the dose
Quadruple the dose
Halve the dosing interval and keep the dose the same, only
Make no change because concentration is independent of dose
items.explanation: With first-order elimination, steady-state concentrations are linearly related to dosing rate, so doubling the desired concentration means doubling the dose. This linear scaling does not hold for zero-order drugs.
items.reference: Concentration of Drugs in Plasma as a Guide to Therapy: "if a doubling of the steady-state plasma concentration is desired, the dose should be doubled."

items.statement: The elimination half-life is the sole determinant of the time required to reach a new steady-state plasma concentration after a change in dosing.
items.explanation: True — half-life determines the time to steady state after starting, changing, or stopping a drug, regardless of route.
items.reference: Clinical Implications of Half-Life Measurements: "it is also the sole determinant of the time required for steady-state plasma concentrations to be achieved after any change in drug dosing."

items.statement: Giving a loading dose shortens the time required to reach a true steady state.
items.explanation: False — a loading dose reaches therapeutic concentrations sooner, but the time to true steady state still depends only on the elimination half-life.
items.reference: Loading Doses: "Nevertheless, the time required for a true steady state to be achieved is still determined only by the elimination half-life."

items.statement: Drug response is related to the free (unbound) rather than the total circulating plasma drug concentration.
items.explanation: True — only unbound drug can distribute to sites of pharmacologic action, so response tracks free drug concentration.
items.reference: Clinical Implications of Altered Protein Binding: "Since only unbound (free) drug can distribute to sites of pharmacologic action, drug response is related to the free rather than the total circulating plasma drug concentration."

items.statement: For drugs eliminated by zero-order kinetics, steady-state plasma concentration increases linearly with the dose.
items.explanation: False — the linear dose-concentration relationship applies to first-order elimination; with zero-order kinetics (a fixed amount eliminated per unit time, e.g., aspirin, fluoxetine), small dose increases produce disproportionate concentration increases.
items.reference: Concentration of Drugs in Plasma as a Guide to Therapy: "This does not apply to drugs eliminated by zero-order kinetics (fixed amount per unit time), where small dosage increases will produce disproportionate increases in plasma concentration."

items.statement: Both excretion into the intestinal lumen and metabolism within the enterocyte decrease oral bioavailability.
items.explanation: True — within the enterocyte, both excretion back into the lumen and metabolism reduce bioavailability, as does subsequent hepatic metabolism or biliary excretion.
items.reference: "First-Pass" Effect: "Both excretion into the intestinal lumen and metabolism decrease bioavailability."

items.statement: Clearance is best defined as the volume of the body cleared of drug per unit time.
items.explanation: True — clearance is defined as volume/time, the volume cleared of drug in a given period.
items.reference: DRUG ELIMINATION: "This defines clearance as volume/time."

items.statement: A wide therapeutic index means there is a large margin between doses that produce a therapeutic effect and those producing toxicity.
items.explanation: True — the therapeutic ratio/index/window is the margin between effective and toxic doses; a wide one indicates a safer drug.
items.reference: Therapeutic Index: "Well-tolerated drugs demonstrate a wide margin, termed the therapeutic ratio, therapeutic index, or therapeutic window, between the doses required to produce a therapeutic effect and those producing toxicity."

items.statement: Drug metabolism always inactivates the drug and never produces pharmacologically active compounds.
items.explanation: False — drug metabolites may exert important pharmacologic activity, and prodrugs depend on metabolism to generate their active forms.
items.reference: DRUG METABOLISM: "Drug metabolites may exert important pharmacologic activity, as discussed further below."

items.statement: When a drug is eliminated by multiple metabolic or excretory pathways, loss of one pathway is much less likely to cause a large change in drug concentration.
items.explanation: True — redundancy across elimination pathways buffers the impact of losing any single one, unlike the high-risk situation of single-pathway dependence.
items.reference: THE CONCEPT OF HIGH-RISK PHARMACOKINETICS: "When drugs undergo elimination by multiple-drug metabolizing or excretory pathways, absence of one pathway (due to a genetic variant or drug interaction) is much less likely to have a large impact on drug concentrations or drug actions."

items.statement: Therapeutic antibodies are very slowly eliminated, allowing infrequent (e.g., monthly) dosing.
items.explanation: True — therapeutic antibodies are very slowly eliminated (probably by lysosomal uptake and degradation), permitting infrequent dosing such as monthly injections.
items.reference: DRUG METABOLISM: "Therapeutic antibodies are very slowly eliminated (allowing infrequent dosing, e.g., monthly injections), probably by lysosomal uptake and degradation."

items.statement: In chronic kidney or liver disease, decreased protein binding can increase drug actions.
items.explanation: True — decreased protein binding raises the free fraction and thus can increase drug actions; acute-phase reactants can transiently increase binding and decrease efficacy.
items.reference: Clinical Implications of Altered Protein Binding: "In chronic kidney or liver disease, protein binding may be decreased and thus drug actions increased."

items.statement: Plasma concentration monitoring is an accurate guide to therapy for every drug.
items.explanation: False — if no relationship between plasma concentration and effect can be established (e.g., when drug access to sites of action outside plasma is highly variable), monitoring plasma concentration may not provide an accurate guide to therapy.
items.reference: Concentration of Drugs in Plasma as a Guide to Therapy: "if no such relationship can be established (e.g., if drug access to important sites of action outside plasma is highly variable), monitoring plasma concentration may not provide an accurate guide to therapy."

items.assertion: Giving a loading dose allows a therapeutic concentration to be reached sooner.
items.reason: A loading dose shortens the elimination half-life of the drug.
items.explanation: The assertion is true (a loading dose reaches therapeutic levels earlier), but the reason is false — a loading dose does not change the elimination half-life; time to true steady state still depends only on the half-life. True assertion + false reason = option 2.
items.reference: Loading Doses: "the time required for a true steady state to be achieved is still determined only by the elimination half-life."

items.assertion: A small reduction in protein binding can substantially increase the effect of a highly protein-bound drug.
items.reason: Only unbound (free) drug can distribute to sites of pharmacologic action.
items.explanation: Both are true and the reason explains the assertion: because only free drug is active, a small fall in binding of a highly bound drug greatly increases the free (active) fraction (e.g., 99%→98% doubles free drug).
items.reference: Clinical Implications of Altered Protein Binding: "Since only unbound (free) drug can distribute to sites of pharmacologic action, drug response is related to the free rather than the total circulating plasma drug concentration."

items.assertion: Nitroglycerin is effective when given by the sublingual route.
items.reason: The sublingual route bypasses presystemic (first-pass) metabolism that would otherwise completely extract the drug.
items.explanation: Both are true and the reason explains the assertion: nitroglycerin is completely extracted presystemically if swallowed, so sublingual (or transdermal/IV) delivery bypasses first-pass metabolism and makes it effective.
items.reference: Clinical Implications of Reduced Bioavailability: "The drug is, therefore, used by the sublingual, transdermal, or intravascular routes, which bypass presystemic metabolism."

items.assertion: A drug whose active form depends on a single metabolic pathway is at high risk of large variability in effect.
items.reason: Any condition that inhibits that single pathway—disease, genetic, or a drug interaction—can dramatically change drug concentrations.
items.explanation: Both are true and the reason explains the assertion: exclusive dependence on one pathway means any inhibitor of it produces marked concentration and effect changes (high-risk pharmacokinetics).
items.reference: THE CONCEPT OF HIGH-RISK PHARMACOKINETICS: "any condition that inhibits that pathway (be it disease related, genetic, or due to a drug interaction) can lead to dramatic changes in drug concentrations and marked variability in drug action."

items.assertion: Clopidogrel's antiplatelet effect can be assumed to end within a few hours of stopping the drug, in line with its ~6-h plasma half-life.
items.reason: Clopidogrel binds irreversibly to platelet P2Y12 receptors.
items.explanation: The assertion is false — the effect lasts 7–10 days (the platelet lifespan), not a few hours. The reason is true and explains why the assertion fails (irreversible binding). False assertion + true reason = option 3.
items.reference: Pharmacodynamics (Time Course of Drug Action): "the antiplatelet effect (which is due to irreversible binding to P2Y12 receptors) persists for the life of the platelet, 7–10 days."

items.assertion: β-blockers should not be stopped abruptly after chronic use.
items.reason: Chronic β-blockade downregulates β-receptor density, leaving too few receptors to respond on withdrawal.
items.explanation: The assertion is true (abrupt withdrawal risks rebound), but the reason is false — chronic β-blockade UPregulates (not downregulates) β-receptor density, and the excess receptors drive rebound effects. True assertion + false reason = option 2.
items.reference: Drug Effects May Be Disease Specific: "β-adrenergic blockers upregulate β-receptor density."

items.assertion: Anticonvulsant and vancomycin concentrations are typically measured at trough.
items.reason: Trough sampling helps ensure a maintained therapeutic effect and limits concentration-dependent toxicity.
items.explanation: Both are true and the reason explains the assertion: trough (predose) levels confirm the drug stays in the therapeutic range and, for vancomycin, limit nephrotoxicity from sustained high levels.
items.reference: Principles of Dose Selection: "concentration should be measured at its lowest during the dosing interval, just prior to a dose at steady state...to ensure a maintained therapeutic effect."

items.assertion: For a first-order drug, doubling the dose is expected to roughly double the steady-state concentration.
items.reason: With first-order elimination, steady-state concentrations are linearly related to the dosing rate.
items.explanation: Both are true and the reason explains the assertion: linear dose-concentration scaling is a property of first-order elimination, so doubling the dose doubles the steady-state concentration.
items.reference: Concentration of Drugs in Plasma as a Guide to Therapy: "the maintenance dose may be adjusted on the basis of the ratio between the desired and measured concentrations at steady state; for example, if a doubling of the steady-state plasma concentration is desired, the dose should be doubled."

items.assertion: It is acceptable to start a drug at a dose highly likely to be effective and down-titrate when the only anticipated side effects are minor.
items.reason: If side effects are minor, the convenience and earlier efficacy can outweigh the small risk of having to reduce the dose.
items.explanation: Both are true and the reason explains the assertion: the start-high-and-down-titrate approach is acceptable specifically when anticipated toxicity is minor (but not when it is serious or life-threatening).
items.reference: PRINCIPLES OF DOSE SELECTION: "If side effects are minor, it may be acceptable to start chronic therapy at a dose highly likely to achieve efficacy and down-titrate if side effects occur."

items.assertion: When a higher dose is needed, increasing the dose while keeping the dosing interval constant is usually preferred over shortening the interval.
items.reason: Regimens that require more frequent dosing are associated with decreased adherence.
items.explanation: Both are true and the reason explains the assertion: increasing the dose at the same interval is usually preferred because more frequent dosing reduces adherence.
items.reference: Concentration of Drugs in Plasma as a Guide to Therapy: "occasionally the dosing interval is shortened to avoid high peak concentrations recognizing that adherence is decreased with regimens requiring frequent dosing."
